---
title: "Reusable Nested Structures"
subtitle: "Build once, instantiate repeatedly, and use nested paths"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

This tutorial builds a diblock, reuses it to make a two-arm star, then reuses
the star to make `starChain`. It assumes the Python environment from the
[diblock tutorial](diblock-copolymer.qmd).

## Construction levels

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: "Three levels of reusable structure construction."
flowchart TB
  D["Level 1: diblock<br/>polyA — polyB"]
  S["Level 2: star<br/>diblock1 + diblock2"]
  C["Level 3: starChain<br/>star1 + star2"]
  D -->|"instantiate twice and link"| S
  S -->|"instantiate twice and link"| C
```

Colons descend into structures. For example,
`star1:diblock2:polyB.end2` addresses `polyB.end2` inside `diblock2` inside
`star1`.

## Python

### 1. Create a world

All reusable graphs live in the same `World`. Later steps instantiate these
graphs without rebuilding their internal links.

```{python}
import pyseb

world = pyseb.World("NestedTutorial")
```

### 2. Build the reusable diblock

Start with `polyA`, then attach `polyB` end to end. Keep the returned graph in
`diblock`; a graph does not need a public structure name before it can be
reused.

```{python}
diblock = world.Add("GaussianPolymer", "polyA")
world.Link("GaussianPolymer", "polyB.end1", "polyA.end2")
```

### 3. Reuse the diblock to make a star

`world.Add(diblock, "diblock1")` creates the first instance. `Link` creates a
second instance and connects the `polyA.end1` reference inside each diblock.
The colon separates a structure instance from a nested subunit path.

```{python}
star = world.Add(diblock, "diblock1")
world.Link(diblock, "diblock2:polyA.end1", "diblock1:polyA.end1")
```

### 4. Reuse the star to make a chain

Repeat the same pattern at the next level. The longer reference paths descend
through a star, a diblock, and finally a polymer reference point.

```{python}
chain = world.Add(star, "star1")
world.Link(
    star,
    "star2:diblock1:polyB.end2",
    "star1:diblock2:polyB.end2",
)
world.Add(chain, "starChain")
```

Naming the final graph exposes `starChain` to the scattering queries.

### 5. Parameterize and evaluate

Repeated instances retain the internal names `polyA` and `polyB`. Consequently
all eight polymers reuse four physical parameters.

```{python}
parameters = {
    "beta_polyA": 1.0,
    "beta_polyB": 0.75,
    "Rg_polyA": 5.0,
    "Rg_polyB": 8.0,
}
q = [0.01, 0.03, 0.1, 0.3]
values = world.EvaluateFormFactor("starChain", parameters, q)

for q_value, value in zip(q, values):
    print(f"q={q_value:g}, F(q)={value:.8f}")
```

The decreasing values show the expected loss of coherence as q resolves the
nested structure's internal length scales.

## C++

```cpp
{{< include cpp/nested-structures.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from _helpers import run_tutorial

run_tutorial("nested-structures")
```

## Next steps

- [Sweep model parameters](parameter-sweeps.qmd)
- [Core concepts](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Core-Concepts)
- [Naming and linking](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Naming-and-Linking)
